Tafseer:
Commentary on Verses 1-2:

الر تِلْكَ آيَاتُ الْكِتَابِ الْحَكِيمِ ۔ أَكَانَ لِلنَّاسِ عَجَبًا أَنْ أَوْحَيْنَا إِلَى رَجُلٍ مِنْهُمْ أَنْ أَنْذِرِ النَّاسَ وَبَشِّرِ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَنَّ لَهُمْ قَدَمَ صِدْقٍ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ قَالَ الْكَافِرُونَ إِنَّ هَذَا لَسَاحِرٌ مُبِينٌ

**Alif, Lam, Ra:** The Quranic name of this Surah is *Alif, Lām, Rā*, and the demonstrative pronoun *tilka* (these) points toward it. These are the isolated letters (*hurūf-e-muqatta'āt*) upon which we carried out a comprehensive discussion at the beginning of the commentary of Al-Baqarah. Describing the Book with the attribute 'wise' (*ḥakīm*) points to the fact that this Book is adorned with proofs and arguments for its every claim, such that it carries its own criterion of truth within itself and is not in need of any external witness. Those who seek a witness for its truth must enter the Book itself; if they possess sound intellect and a healthy nature, its wisdom will clear up their every doubt. 'Have the people been amazed...'—the context proves that 'the people' (*al-nās*) here refers to the Quraysh. That is: when this Book is in itself a proof and argument, how does the mere fact that its revelation came down upon a man from among themselves become a reason for denial? The coming of this revelation upon a man from among themselves is not a matter of denial or amazement; rather, if they reflect, it is a great favor upon them that He revealed this Book through a man from among themselves, whose past, present, character, and conduct they know well, and whose trustworthiness and truthfulness they have acknowledged. Would it have been better if an outsider had borne witness to this truth, or is it better that Allah chose one of their best individuals to be a witness to this truth, so their own language and conscience completed the proof against them? It would have been completely unreasonable to select an angel or jinn for this task. For humans, the teacher, guide, model, and example can only be a human, not angels or jinn.

**The Core Message of the Book and its Bearer:** 'that: "Warn mankind and give good tidings to those who believe..."' This reports the core message of this Book and its bearer. The meaning is: this message is also not a matter of amazement or denial, but is a reality. This worldly life is not to end in vain; rather, after it, a Day is to come when everyone must return to God, and on that Day, those who believed in this Book will find a station of honor and elevation before God, while those who denied it will be humiliated and cast into Hell. If this reality hurts the pride of the arrogant leaders of the Quraysh, let it, and if they are amazed, let them be; but a reality does not become false because it strikes at the pride of a group. In 'a foot of truthfulness' (*qadama ṣidqin*), the word 'truthfulness' (*ṣidq*), as we explained elsewhere, points to firmness, stability, and established position; therefore, the meaning of 'a foot of truthfulness' is a station of honor, an elevated rank, a high position, and eternal success. While the glad tiding for the believers is described in explicit words in this verse, the aspect of warning is left ambiguous. In our view, the reason is that in this group of Surahs, the dominant aspect is the glad tiding for the believers. The fate of the disbelievers is mentioned not primarily, but secondarily. 'The disbelievers say, "Indeed, this is an obvious magician."' This is the expression of the disbelievers' amazement and denial. The meaning is: when the wise verses of this Book are recited to them, instead of appreciating them and believing, they declare its bringer an obvious magician. That is: they attribute the power, effect, eloquence, and beauty of this Book to magic and sorcery, telling their common people: do not assume that the effect and attraction you feel in this Book and his words is because it came from God or contains wisdom; rather, just as a sorcerer makes a thing appear as something else through his tricks, this man (Muhammad, peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is a master of verbal sorcery, presenting his words in a manner that simple people are mesmerized by them.

**A Specific Aspect of Calling the Prophets Magicians and Soothsayers:** It should be kept in mind that the disbelievers called the prophets magicians not only to diminish the significance of their miracles, but they used this tool also to diminish the value of their words. When they saw the healthy-natured among them being affected by the words presented by the Prophet, they fanned the rumor: this is merely verbal sorcery, protect yourselves from this deceit. In calling the Quran itself magic, this was their purpose. Similarly, the disbelievers called the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) a soothsayer (*kāhin*). Through this, too, they aimed to turn their common people's attention away from the Quran and the Prophet—claiming that this book is not an extraordinary thing, and its bringer is not an extraordinary being. Just as our soothsayers present their words in a rhymed and polished style, and just as their words carry some predictions regarding the future, his words carry them too. The meaning is: these are well-known things to us; there is no reason to give them a divine status.

Commentary on Verses 3-4:

اِنَّ رَبَّكُمُ اللّٰهُ الَّذِيْ خَلَقَ السَّمٰوٰتِ وَالْاَرْضِ فِيْ سِتَّةِ اَيَّامٍ ثُمَّ اسْتَوٰى عَلَي الْعَرْشِ يُدَبِّرُ الْاَمْرَ ۭمَا مِنْ شَفِيْعٍ اِلَّا مِنْۢ بَعْدِ اِذْنِهٖ ۭ ذٰلِكُمُ اللّٰهُ رَبُّكُمْ فَاعْبُدُوْهُ ۭ اَفَلَا تَذَكَّرُوْنَ ۔ اِلَيْهِ مَرْجِعُكُمْ جَمِيْعًا ۭوَعْدَ اللّٰهِ حَقًّا ۭ اِنَّهٗ يَبْدَؤُا الْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيْدُهٗ لِيَجْزِيَ الَّذِيْنَ اٰمَنُوْا وَعَمِلُوا الصّٰلِحٰتِ بِالْقِسْطِ ۭ وَالَّذِيْنَ كَفَرُوْا لَھُمْ شَرَابٌ مِّنْ حَمِيْمٍ وَّعَذَابٌ اَلِيْمٌۢ بِمَا كَانُوْا يَكْسِبُوْنَ 

**The Proof for the Mentioned Warning and Glad Tiding:** This explains the proof for the warning and glad tiding mentioned above, and the order of premises is as follows: the Allah who created the heavens and the earth—whose being the Creator is acknowledged by you too—is your Lord, Master, and Ally. How is it possible that the Creator is one, but the Lord and Master becomes someone else? If you have made other lords, attaching hopes to them, this is merely your foolishness, which is contrary to reason, nature, and your own acknowledged premise. Furthermore, this universe did not appear as an accidental mishap, but was developed gradually in six days—or in other words, six epochs—according to God's days. This gradual order and development is witness that its appearance is not an accidental mishap, nor is it a plaything, but is a highly organized and purposeful system; and the purposefulness of this system necessitates that man, who holds the status of a vicegerent in this universe, is not left uncontrolled; rather, a Day must come when those who spent their lives in accordance with the Giver's pleasure receive their reward with full justice, and those who took this world as a plaything, spending their lives in vanity, face the punishment for their crimes. 'then He established Himself above the Throne, directing the matter.' That is: do not assume that God created the world and then sat aside as a silent primary cause (*ʿillat-e-ʿūlā*), leaving the entire administration to others; rather, He Himself is established on the Throne of governance, directing all matters. In the Psalms, this reality is expressed in the words: 'For thou hast maintained my right and my cause; thou satest in the throne judging right.' (9:4). 'There is no intercessor except after His permission.' That is: let no one be in the misunderstanding that they can save themselves from God's justice through anyone's intercession. Before Him, no one will have the courage to open their mouth for intercession without His permission, nor can any intercession turn falsehood into truth or truth into falsehood. Allah's knowledge encompasses everything. 'That is Allah, your Lord, so worship Him. Then will you not remember?'—that is: this Allah, who is the Creator of the heavens and the earth, established on the Throne of governance to rule with justice, before whom no intercessor can dare intercede without His permission, is your Lord; therefore, worship Him alone. Where did you make other lords from? How do you ignore your own acknowledged premises? 'To Him is your return all together. [It is] the promise of Allah in truth.' To Him is the return of all. There is no other ally or return. This is the certain promise of Allah. The emphasis in 'all together' (*ǧamīʿan*) is to show: both you and those you made His partners and intercessors must be presented before God; no one can escape. 'Indeed, He begins creation; then He repeats it' is the proof for His promise being in truth: He begins creation, and He will repeat it; why should He face any difficulty in repeating it when He began it in the first instance? This also indicates: those who have no share in the beginning of creation nor in its repeating—on what basis have you made them lords and allies?

**The Primary Purpose of the Day of Resurrection:** 'that He may reward those who believed and did righteous deeds in justice.' This is the primary purpose of the Day of Resurrection. This shows that the primary purpose of Resurrection is to reward the believers; punishing the disbelievers is among its accessories. This is the glad tiding mentioned at the beginning. Having explained its proof, He pointed to it again.

**The Initial Food of Hospitality for the Disbelievers:** 'But those who disbelieved will have a drink of scalding water and a painful punishment because they used to disbelieve.' This describes the punishment the disbelievers will face. A 'drink of scalding water' (*šarābun min ḥamīmin*), as we explained elsewhere, is mentioned as the initial food of hospitality—meaning: their first reception upon entering will be with scalding water. After that, the doors of a painful punishment will be opened for them.

Commentary on Verses 5-10:

هُوَ الَّذِي جَعَلَ الشَّمْسَ ضِيَاءً وَالْقَمَرَ نُورًا وَقَدَّرَهُ مَنَازِلَ لِتَعْلَمُوا عَدَدَ السِّنِينَ وَالْحِسَابَ مَا خَلَقَ اللَّهُ ذَلِكَ إِلا بِالْحَقِّ يُفَصِّلُ الآيَاتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ * إِنَّ فِي اخْتِلافِ اللَّيْلِ وَالنَّهَارِ وَمَا خَلَقَ اللَّهُ فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالأرْضِ لآيَاتٍ لِقَوْمٍ يَتَّقُونَ * إِنَّ الَّذِينَ لا يَرْجُونَ لِقَاءَنَا وَرَضُوا بِالْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا وَاطْمَأَنُّوا بِهَا وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ عَنْ آيَاتِنَا غَافِلُونَ * أُولَئِكَ مَأْوَاهُمُ النَّارُ بِمَا كَانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ * إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ يَهْدِيهِمْ رَبُّهُمْ بِإِيمَانِهِمْ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهِمُ الأنْهَارُ فِي جَنَّاتِ النَّعِيمِ * دَعْوَاهُمْ فِيهَا سُبْحَانَكَ اللَّهُمَّ وَتَحِيَّتُهُمْ فِيهَا سَلَامٌ وَآخِرُ دَعْوَاهُمْ أَنِ الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ

**The Proof for the Certainty of Reward and Punishment:** After monotheism, this explains the proof for the certainty of reward and punishment: whoever reflects on this universe will clearly see that this is not a plaything, but in its every corner, its Creator's power, mercy, and lordship are displayed. It possesses a highly elaborate arrangement and order. The sun rises in accordance with a precise system, illuminating the world. Through it, different seasons are produced, every one of which is necessary for the life and survival of our world. The chandy (moon) receives light from it, traversing its determined phases to serve as a light for our dark nights and as a calendar for our months and years. Who can say that this world will continue without any distinction between good and evil, or end in vain? If this were assumed, this power and wisdom displayed in every corner of the universe would become empty of purpose. Then, this world would be completely false and a plaything; and this is such an unreasonable, unjust, and unnatural thing that no sane human can believe it. If some bricks are lying scattered, we can say they are lying in vain; but can we harbor the same assumption regarding the Taj Mahal, the Red Fort, or the mosques of Lahore and Delhi?

In this verse, the sun is described as *ḍiyā'* (brilliant light) and the moon as *nūr* (cool light). Some have explained this difference by stating that the sun's light is its own, while the moon's light is received from the sun. Although this is correct in itself, in my view, *ḍiyā'* includes the concept of heat alongside light, while *nūr* is cool light. And it is a fact that the sun's light has heat, while the moon's light is cool.

**The Acknowledgment of Healthy Human Nature:** 'Allah did not create all this except in truth'—this is the acknowledgment of healthy human nature: the wise person who reflects on this universe cries out: this is not false, but was created with a great purpose, and this purpose necessitates that it end in a manner that decides between truth and falsehood with full justice, rewarding or punishing everyone according to their deeds. 'He details the verses for a people who know.' He stated: the proofs and signs of this reward and punishment are spread within this universe, which cannot remain hidden from seeing eyes; but if they were hidden from some, We have explained their details for those who wish to know and understand. This same concept is expressed in Surah Ali 'Imran, verse 191: 'and reflect on the creation of the heavens and the earth, [saying], "Our Lord, You did not create this aimlessly; exalted are You [above such a thing]; then protect us from the punishment of the Fire."' Those seeking details must also refer to the commentary of that verse.

**The Lesson of the Alternation of Night and Day:** 'Indeed, in the alternation of the night and the day...' points to their constant pursuit of one another, which reveals that this is not in vain, but will end in a great result; secondly, it points to that system of lordship hidden within the alternation of the night and day: the day provides the field for man's livelihood, and the night bended his bed for rest. Whoever reflects on this system inevitably concludes that this harmony among opposites can exist only when it is believed that this entire universe operates under the will of a single, All-Powerful Creator, and then it is derived that He who established this system of lordship will not leave man uncontrolled; rather, a Day must come when those who recognized His lordship receive their reward, and those who ignored it are cast into Hell. This is the conclusion derived from reflecting on all components of the universe, and this is what guides man to the Hereafter, producing that true piety (*taqwa*) pointed to in the words 'exalted are You [above such a thing]; then protect us from the punishment of the Fire' in Ali 'Imran.

**The Fate of Those Who Close Their Eyes to His Signs:** 'Indeed, those who do not expect...'—this describes the fate of those who remain blind to His signs, satisfied with this worldly life, fearing neither His meeting nor His punishment. He stated: their refuge will be Hell. 'Expect' (*yarǧūna*) is used here in the sense of expecting or fearing, which is its original literal meaning.

**The Fate of the Believers:** 'Indeed, those who believed and did righteous deeds - their Lord will guide them...' 'Guidance' (*yahdīhim*) here is used in the sense of guidance to the destination. That is: because of their faith, Allah will guide them to their respective ranks and stations in Paradise. 'Their call therein will be, "Exalted are You, O Allah,"'—when they see with their eyes that truth is established and falsehood abolished, they will cry out: 'Exalted are You, O Allah,' because seeing His signs confirmed their expectation that this universe is not a plaything, and it is far from His glory to create anything in vain. 'and their greeting therein will be, "Peace."'—like a successful team, they will greet and congratulate one another, while the disbelievers will be cursing one another. 'And the last of their call will be, "Praise to Allah, Lord of the worlds!"'—this is gratitude upon the completion of favor: when they see blessings on all sides, they will utter this praise.

The upcoming subject (Verses 11 to 19): Next, He answered the disbelievers' demand for punishment: if reward and punishment are certain, and we deserve it because of our deeds, why does He not show us some example of this punishment? In response, He referred to His law: He is quick in mercy, but slow in punishment. If He were quick in punishment just as He is in mercy, their term would have been ended long ago. Then, He clarified the reality of man's haste: he demands punishment insolently, but when he falls under apprehension, he begins to wail, and as soon as he receives respite, he exults again. Such people never accept guidance. Then, He pointed to history: their predecessor nations, whose successors they became, provide lessons; why do they not learn from them, and why do they wish to face the same end? Afterward, He answered their demand to change the Quran or make it soft: they were not ready to hear monotheism, reward, and punishment; hence, they demanded: 'bring a Quran other than this or change it.' In response, the Prophet was commanded to say: this is not my word that I could alter; this is a responsibility laid upon me by God. If I could escape it, I would not have mentioned it. I spent a long life among you, and you know I never sought authority; how then can I commit this slander against God in this age? Finally, He explained the absurdity of polytheism: what they defend is merely a false concept, which has no existence in the heavens or the earth. In this light, recite the verses:

وَلَوْ يُعَجِّلُ اللّٰهُ لِلنَّاسِ الشَّرَّ اسْتِعْجَالَھُمْ بِالْخَيْرِ لَقُضِيَ اِلَيْهِمْ اَجَلُھُمْ ۭ فَنَذَرُ الَّذِيْنَ لَا يَرْجُوْنَ لِقَاۗءَنَا فِيْ طُغْيَانِهِمْ يَعْمَهُوْنَ 

**Answering Their Haste for Punishment:** This is the answer to their haste for punishment. When they were warned of the end of their denial, they, to check the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), would demand: if you are a true messenger, and your denial deserves punishment, do not delay it. We are anxious to see it. In response, He stated: His law is that He is quick in mercy, but not in punishment. In 'their haste for good' (*istiʿǧālahum bi-al-ḫayri*), the verbal noun is grammatically construct with its object. That is: since He created this universe for His mercy, He bestows His mercy upon people, and desires that they appreciate His mercy before the opportunity ends, choosing the path of goodness. His law demands that He does not hasten the punishment for those who exult and deny His Messenger. 'so We leave those who do not expect Our meeting to wander blindly in their transgression.' This describes the requirement of His law: it demands that despite their disobedience, He grants them respite, so they may reform if any capacity remains, and if they do not, the proof is completed against them, leaving them no excuse when they are presented on the Day of Resurrection, of which they are completely negligent.

وَاِذَا مَسَّ الْاِنْسَانَ الضُّرُّ دَعَانَا لِجَنْۢبِهٖٓ اَوْ قَاعِدًا اَوْ قَاۗىِٕمًا ۚ فَلَمَّا كَشَفْنَا عَنْهُ ضُرَّهٗ مَرَّ كَاَنْ لَّمْ يَدْعُنَآ اِلٰى ضُرٍّ مَّسَّهٗ ۭ كَذٰلِكَ زُيِّنَ لِلْمُسْرِفِيْنَ مَا كَانُوْا يَعْمَلُوْنَ 

**The Nature of the Rebellious:** Although 'man' (*al-insān*) is general, it refers to those rebellious leaders of the Quraysh who demanded punishment. He did not address them directly to express indifference, stating the matter in general words. This is a blow to their pride, and points to the reality: let no one assume that if their demand for punishment is fulfilled, they will believe. Such people do not believe; rather, when they fall under apprehension, they cry out to God, making covenants of reform; but as soon as they receive respite, they return to their exultation, forgetting that they were ever apprehended and cried to God to escape. 'Thus is made pleasing to the transgressors that which they used to do'—that is: this description fits these rebellious ones who transgress His limits. Under His law, their evil deeds are made pleasing to them, so even if they are shown a sign of punishment, they will return to their mud as soon as they escape.

Commentary on Verses 13-14:

وَوَلَقَدْ اَهْلَكْنَا الْقُرُوْنَ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ لَمَّا ظَلَمُوْا ۙ وَجَاۗءَتْھُمْ رُسُلُھُمْ بِالْبَيِّنٰتِ وَمَا كَانُوْا لِيُؤْمِنُوْا ۭ كَذٰلِكَ نَجْزِي الْقَوْمَ الْمُجْرِمِيْنَ ۔ ثُمَّ جَعَلْنٰكُمْ خَلٰۗىِٕفَ فِي الْاَرْضِ مِنْۢ بَعْدِهمْ لِنَنْظُرَ كَيْفَ تَعْمَلُوْنَ 

**The Witness of History:** This addresses the Quraysh directly in the light of history: why do you not learn from what befell others in this very land? Why do you think it is necessary that you also face it to understand that what was said to you was true? The reference here is brief. Ahead in this Surah, some important historical events are detailed. 'when they wronged' (*lammā thalamū*) refers to the wrong they committed against themselves by denying their messengers. 'and their messengers had come to them with clear proofs, and they were not to believe' is the detail of this wrong. 'Thus do We recompense the criminal people'—meaning: those who deny their messengers, We punish them in this manner. The meaning is: when you commit the same crime, how will you escape the punishment? 'Then We made you successors in the land...'—meaning: since you became their successors, why should your dealing be different from theirs? He gave you their place to see what you do.

وَإِذَا تُتْلَى عَلَيْهِمْ آيَاتُنَا بَيِّنَاتٍ قَالَ الَّذِينَ لا يَرْجُونَ لِقَاءَنَا ائْتِ بِقُرْآنٍ غَيْرِ هَذَا أَوْ بَدِّلْهُ قُلْ مَا يَكُونُ لِي أَنْ أُبَدِّلَهُ مِنْ تِلْقَاءِ نَفْسِي إِنْ أَتَّبِعُ إِلا مَا يُوحَى إِلَيَّ إِنِّي أَخَافُ إِنْ عَصَيْتُ رَبِّي عَذَابَ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ 

That is: when these clear verses of monotheism and the Hereafter are recited to them, they recoil from them, demanding: 'Bring a Quran other than this or change it.' This demand was proof of their defeat. They had realized that they could not escape the Quran. Now, they attempted to seek some change in it so it would be acceptable to them. In response, the Prophet was commanded to say: this is not my own composition that I could alter; I merely follow what is revealed to me. If I make any alteration in it, what will I answer to God on that Day?

Commentary on Verses 16-17:

قُلْ لَوْ شَاءَ اللَّهُ مَا تَلَوْتُهُ عَلَيْكُمْ وَلا أَدْرَاكُمْ بِهِ فَقَدْ لَبِثْتُ فِيكُمْ عُمُرًا مِنْ قَبْلِهِ أَفَلا تَعْقِلُونَ * فَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ افْتَرَى عَلَى اللَّهِ كَذِبًا أَوْ كَذَّبَ بِآيَاتِهِ إِنَّهُ لا يُفْلِحُ الْمُجْرِمُونَ 

*Adrākum* is from *darā/yadrī*, in the causative form (*af'al*), in the third person singular. The subject is Allah. Some have translated it as the first person to align it with *mā talawtuhu*, but this is contrary to Arabic grammar.

**A Decisive Answer:** This is the answer to the first part of their demand—namely: 'bring a Quran other than this.' He stated: you assume I presented this Quran out of my own desire to establish my leadership. This is wrong. I never desired this heavy burden. It was His will that I carry it. If He had not willed, I would not have presented it, nor would He have informed you through it.

'for I had remained among you a lifetime before it. Then will you not reason?' **An Argument From the Prophet's Life:** This is a simple and decisive argument that no fair person can deny. He stated: I am no stranger to you, whose past and present are unknown. I spent forty years of my life among you. In this long period, did you ever experience any lie or deceit from me? Did you ever find any desire for leadership within me? A person's character is not built overnight, nor is it hidden from those around him. The one who never lied to people—how can he commit this great slander against God? Yesterday you called me truthful and trustworthy; how did I become a liar overnight? O servants of God, use reason and reflect without prejudice. This shows that before prophethood, the prophets' lives are so clean that their opponents find no room to criticize, and this is a great proof for their truthfulness. 'So who is more unjust than he who invents a lie against Allah...'—meaning: if I am inventing a lie, claiming prophethood falsely, who can be more unjust than me? And if I am true, and you deny His verses, who can be more unjust than you? The future will decide who is unjust. Those who are criminals will never succeed. It should be kept in mind that the clash between the messengers and their opponents always ends in the victory of the truth, because the messenger is God's court, and victory is guaranteed for him in this world and the Hereafter.

Commentary on Verses 18-19:

وَيَعْبُدُوْنَ مِنْ دُوْنِ اللّٰهِ مَا لَا يَضُرُّهُمْ وَلَا يَنْفَعُهُمْ وَيَقُوْلُوْنَ هٰٓؤُلَاءِ شُفَعَآؤُنَا عِنْدَ اللّٰهِ ۭ قُلْ اَتُنَبِّئُوْنَ اللّٰهَ بِمَا لَا يَعْلَمُ فِي السَّمٰوٰتِ وَلَا فِي الْاَرْضِ ۭ سُبْحٰنَهُ وَتَعٰلٰى عَمَّا يُشْرِكُوْنَ ۔ وَمَا كَانَ النَّاسُ اِلَّآ اُمَّةً وَّاحِدَةً فَاخْتَلَفُوْا ۭ وَلَوْلَا كَلِمَةٌ سَبَقَتْ مِنْ رَّبِّكَ لَقُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ فِيْمَا فِيْهِ يَخْتَلِفُوْنَ 

**The Primary Source of the Polytheists' Anger:** 'And they worship besides Allah...' This refers to what made them recoil from the Quran, and is an example of their slander, showing that they are the greatest wrongdoers: they worship things that can neither harm nor benefit them, claiming: 'These are our intercessors before Allah; whatever we receive in this world—wealth, children, provision—is through their intercession, and they will save us in the Hereafter.' It should be kept in mind that the Arabs did not believe their deities were partners in His Being or were creators, but believed they were His beloved ones, who would intercede for them. 

**An Elegant Style of Expression:** 'Say, "Do you inform Allah of something He does not know in the heavens or on the earth?"' This refutes their intercessors on the principle of negating a thing by negating its necessary consequence. That is: if these intercessors had any existence in the heavens or the earth, Allah would have known them, as they are His beloved ones. But God knows nothing of them; you are the ones who are inventing them. A parallel exists in Imru' al-Qays's poetry: 'it is not guided by its signs'—meaning: the signs do not exist. This style is also present in Surah Ar-Ra'd, verse 33.

**A Proof Against Polytheism:** 'Exalted is He and high above what they associate with Him.' *Subhānahu* is not merely a word of purification, but is a great proof against polytheism. That is: associating anything with God that possesses attributes contrary to His divine attributes is contrary to reason, because it negates His established attributes.

**The Witness of History for Monotheism:** 'And mankind was not but one nation, but they differed.' This is the historical proof: Allah sent His prophets to teach only monotheism, making everyone a single nation. But people later created differences, choosing various paths of deviation. The meaning is: do not take the presence of various paths of deviation as proof that they are from God. They have no connection to God, but are the inventions of those who went astray. This also refuted the modern theory that man began with polytheism and progressed to monotheism. The Quran states the contrary: He taught monotheism first, but people created differences. We refuted this theory in our book *The Reality of Monotheism*. 'And if not for a word...'—meaning: the Day of Resurrection has been established to decide this difference. If it were not, He would have decided it today.

The upcoming subject (Verses 20 to 27): Next, He referred to their demand for a sign of punishment, explaining: they demand a sign of punishment, claiming that if they are shown one, they will believe; but when they are shown a sign, they pray to God only as long as they are caught in it, and as soon as they escape, they return to their exultation. What is the benefit of showing a sign to such people? Then, He refuted their delusion: because your circumstances are favorable today, with abundance of means, it does not mean you have escaped His apprehension. His punishment falls upon a nation when they are so magon in their circumstances that they assume no danger can strike them. In this light, recite the verses:

وَيَقُوْلُوْنَ لَوْلَآ اُنْزِلَ عَلَيْهِ اٰيَةٌ مِّنْ رَّبِّهٖ ۚ فَقُلْ اِنَّمَا الْغَيْبُ لِلّٰهِ فَانْتَظِرُوْا ۚ اِنِّىْ مَعَكُمْ مِّنَ الْمُنْتَظِرِيْنَ 

**Answering Their Haste for Punishment:** 'And they say, "Why is a sign not sent down to him from his Lord?"' 'A sign' (*āyah*) here refers to a sign of punishment, as the context indicates. The Quran was warning them of punishment in this world and the Hereafter. This struck at their pride; hence, they would demand: if you are true, why does this punishment not appear? We are anxious to see it. In response, He commanded: say that this is from God, and I merely warn you; as for when and how it will come, these are matters of the unseen (*al-ġayb*), known only to Allah. 'So wait; indeed, I am with you among those who wait.' If you want to see it, wait. I am also waiting for it based on the news He gave. This waiting does not imply that the Prophet desired to see his nation destroyed; the prophets exert all their power to save their nations, but when the nation accumulates the causes of destruction, the Prophet's heart is also in constant expectation of His command. This waiting is filled with grief and sorrow.

وَاِذَآ اَذَقْــنَا النَّاسَ رَحْمَةً مِّنْۢ بَعْدِ ضَرَّاۗءَ مَسَّتْهُمْ اِذَا لَهُمْ مَّكْرٌ فِيْٓ اٰيَاتِنَا ۭ قُلِ اللّٰهُ اَسْرَعُ مَكْرًا ۭ اِنَّ رُسُلَنَا يَكْتُبُوْنَ مَا تَمْكُرُوْنَ 

This describes the state of the Quraysh, though expressed in general words to show indifference, and to reveal that everyone in this state behaves similarly. He stated: the effect of these warning signs remains only as long as they are caught in them. As soon as they escape, their leaders invent various philosophies to satisfy their common people, returning to their exultation. 'at once they plan against Our verses'—the word 'plan' (*makr*) was discussed under Surah Ali 'Imran, meaning a secret design or trick. An example of their plot is mentioned in the subsequent verse: '"If You should save us from this, we will surely be among the thankful." But when He saved them, at once they committed insolence upon the earth...' Another example is in: '"Our fathers were also touched by adversity and ease,"'—meaning: their leaders would explain: 'these soft and hard times are common in the lives of nations, and our ancestors faced them too; there is no need to assume this has any connection to our deeds.' 'Say, "Allah is swifter in planning."' If you wish to plot, go ahead; His design is swifter and so firm that no one can escape. 'Indeed, Our messengers [i.e., angels] write down what you plan'—meaning: Our angels are writing down your every plot.

Commentary on Verses 22-23:

هُوَ الَّذِيْ يُسَيِّرُكُمْ فِي الْبَرِّ وَالْبَحْرِ ۭ حَتّٰى اِذَا كُنْتُمْ فِي الْفُلْكِ ۚ وَجَرَيْنَ بِهِمْ بِرِيْحٍ طَيِّبَةٍ وَّفَرِحُوْا بِهَا جَاۗءَتْهَا رِيْحٌ عَاصِفٌ وَّجَاۗءَهُمُ الْمَوْجُ مِنْ كُلِّ مَكَانٍ وَّظَنُّوْٓا اَنَّهُمْ اُحِيْطَ بِهِمْ ۙ دَعَوُا اللّٰهَ مُخْلِصِيْنَ لَهُ الدِّيْنَ ڬ لَىِٕنْ اَنْجَيْـتَنَا مِنْ هٰذِهٖ لَنَكُوْنَنَّ مِنَ الشّٰكِرِيْنَ ۔ فَلَمَّآ اَنْجٰهُمْ اِذَا هُمْ يَبْغُوْنَ فِي الْاَرْضِ بِغَيْرِ الْحَقِّ ۭ يٰٓاَيُّهَا النَّاسُ اِنَّمَا بَغْيُكُمْ عَلٰٓي اَنْفُسِكُمْ ۙ مَّتَاعَ الْحَيٰوةِ الدُّنْيَا ۡ ثُمَّ اِلَيْنَا مَرْجِعُكُمْ فَنُنَبِّئُكُمْ بِمَا كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُوْنَ 

*Yusayyirukum*—*tasyīr* means to drive or cause to travel. Since all means and wisdom are from Him, He stated: He causes you to travel, so your eyes do not remain stuck in means but reach the Creator. *Al-fulk* (ship) is used as masculine, feminine, singular, and plural; here, it is followed by the plural verb *ǧarayna*. *Birīḥin ṭayyibatin* means a favorable wind; its contrary is *rīḥun ʿāṣifun* (a storm wind). The address shifted here to the third person singular, because this is a metaphor, and the third person is more appropriate for a metaphor. 'they commit insolence upon the earth without right'—*baġy* (insolence) is the result of arrogance, which is appropriate only for the Creator; for a creature in need of His mercy, exulting is completely without right. 'O mankind'addresses the Quraysh, and 'enjoyment' (*matā'a*) is in the accusative case due to an omitted verb.

**A Highly Realistic Metaphor:** This is the metaphor of what was stated above: when we show mercy to a person after distress, he plots against Us. He compared them to passengers on a ship: when the wind is favorable and the ship moves smoothly, they rejoice; but suddenly, a storm wind rises and waves strike from all sides, so they think they are encompassed in destruction. In this moment, they pray to God: 'If You should save us from this, we will surely be among the thankful.' But when He saves them, they return to their insolence upon the earth. The meaning is: those who demand punishment are in this state. Today, their ship moves smoothly, which is why they are exulting. When they are warned of His apprehension, they do not understand, challenging the Prophet; but when they are caught, they will cry out, and return to their exultation as soon as they escape. Such people benefit from no warning. They are left for the decisive punishment.

**Threatening the Quraysh:** 'O mankind, your insolence is only against yourselves...'—your insolence will not harm God or His Messenger, but will harm yourselves. Every insolence of yours completes the proof against you, bringing you closer to the decisive punishment. '[it is only] the enjoyment of worldly life'—enjoy it for a few days, but remember that your return is to Us, and We will place your deeds before you, and no intercessor of yours will benefit you.

اِنَّمَا مَثَلُ الْحَيٰوةِ الدُّنْيَا كَمَاۗءٍ اَنْزَلْنٰهُ مِنَ السَّمَاۗءِ فَاخْتَلَطَ بِهٖ نَبَاتُ الْاَرْضِ مِمَّا يَاْكُلُ النَّاسُ وَالْاَنْعَامُ ۭﱑ اِذَآ اَخَذَتِ الْاَرْضُ زُخْرُفَهَا وَازَّيَّنَتْ وَظَنَّ اَهْلُهَا اَنَّهُمْ قٰدِرُوْنَ عَلَيْهَا ۙ اَتٰهَا اَمْرُنَا لَيْلًا اَوْ نَهَارًا فَجَعَلْنٰهَا حَصِيْدًا كَاَنْ لَّمْ تَغْنِ بِالْاَمْسِ ۭ كَذٰلِكَ نُفَصِّلُ الْاٰيٰتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَّتَفَكَّرُوْنَ 

**The Metaphor of Worldly Life:** 'The example of [this] worldly life...'—this is the metaphor of worldly life, which was referred to as 'the enjoyment of worldly life' (*matā'a al-ḥayāti al-dunyā*) above, with which the disbelievers are pleased. 'so that the plants of the earth absorb it...' (*faẖtalaṭa bihi nabātu al-arḍi*)—*iẖtilāṭ* means mixing and growing dense. This is a description of the dense growth of crops after rain. 'of which men and cattle eat'—all kinds of plants grew, both for humans and cattle. 'until, when the earth has taken on its adornment and is beautified'—*zuḫruf* means beauty and adornment. *Izzayyanat* and *tazayyanat* carry the same meaning. 'and its people think that they have capability over it'—when the crops are at their height, the owners rejoice, assuming they have won the game. 'there comes to it Our command by night or by day, and We make it as a harvest clean-cut'—'Our command' (*amrunā*) here means the punishment. 'by night or by day'—whenever We willed, Our punishment fell upon it. 'as if it had not flourished yesterday'—as if it never existed.

**The Punishment Falls at the Height of Prosperity:** The meaning is: do not challenge God in your pride. Just as rain falls, crops grow, and then a cold wind destroys them suddenly, We will apprehend you at the height of your prosperity, and you will not be able to do anything. If you wish to see His signs, We are explaining them; let those who possess intellect reflect. A wise person learns from others, instead of waiting for the punishment to fall upon him. In Surah Al-Qalam, verses 17-26, the story of the owners of the garden explains this. This also shows that a corrupt nation is apprehended at the height of its prosperity, because when a nation is morally dead, its prosperity only hastens its ruin.

Commentary on Verses 25-27:

وَاللّٰهُ يَدْعُوْٓا اِلٰى دَارِ السَّلٰمِ ۭ وَيَهْدِيْ مَنْ يَّشَاۗءُ اِلٰى صِرَاطٍ مُّسْتَــقِيْمٍ * لِلَّذِيْنَ اَحْسَـنُوا الْحُسْنٰى وَزِيَادَةٌ ۭ وَلَا يَرْهَقُ وُجُوْهَهُمْ قَتَرٌ وَّلَا ذِلَّةٌ ۭ اُولٰۗىِٕكَ اَصْحٰبُ الْجَنَّةِ ۚ هُمْ فِيْهَا خٰلِدُوْنَ * وَالَّذِيْنَ كَسَبُوا السَّيِّاٰتِ جَزَاۗءُ سَـيِّئَةٍۢ بِمِثْلِهَا ۙ وَتَرْهَقُهُمْ ذِلَّةٌ ۭ مَا لَهُمْ مِّنَ اللّٰهِ مِنْ عَاصِمٍ ۚ كَاَنَّمَآ اُغْشِيَتْ وُجُوْهَهُمْ قِطَعًا مِّنَ الَّيْلِ مُظْلِمًا ۭ اُولٰۗىِٕكَ اَصْحٰبُ النَّارِ ۚ هُمْ فِيْهَا خٰلِدُوْن 

'And Allah invites to the Home of Peace'—*salām* means peace and comfort. This refers to Paradise, because there, no past grief or future fear exists, but only eternal peace. This word stands in opposition to the worldly life on which they are pleased. 'and He guides whom He wills to a straight path'—this path is not for everyone; He grants its facilitation to whom He wills according to His law of justice and wisdom. 'For those who have done good is the best [reward] and extra'—this describes His dealing with them in Paradise: they will receive their reward, and His extra favor. This extra favor is explained in another place: 'Whoever comes [on the Day of Judgement] with a good deed will have ten times the like thereof.' (Al-An'am: 160). 'and darkness will not cover their faces, nor humiliation'—*rahaqa* means to cover or dominate, and *qatar* means darkness or dust. The context indicates that it refers to that darkness of despair which covers the face. Negating these implies confirming their contraries—namely: their faces will be bright and joyful. 'But they who have earned evil deeds - the recompense of an evil deed is the like thereof, and humiliation will cover them. They will have from Allah no protector.' This describes the fate of the wicked: their punishment will be equal to their deeds, without any injustice. Humidity and darkness are described in: 'as if their faces had been covered with pieces of the night so dark.' Since this is the darkness of eternal despair, their faces will appear as if covered with pieces of the dark night. 'They will have from Allah no protector' is a parenthetical clause—meaning: no one will save them from His punishment, and those they worshiped as intercessors will disappear.

The upcoming subject (Verses 28 to 36): Above, in 'They will have from Allah no protector,' He pointed to the reality that their deities will not save them; now, He detailed their state on the Day of Resurrection, explaining that those expectations they harbored based on mere assumptions will be destroyed. Recite the verses:

وَيَوْمَ نَحْشُرُهُمْ جَمِيْعًا ثُمَّ نَقُوْلُ لِلَّذِيْنَ اَشْرَكُوْا مَكَانَكُمْ اَنْتُمْ وَشُرَكَاۗؤُكُمْ ۚفَزَيَّلْنَا بَيْنَهُمْ وَقَالَ شُرَكَاۗؤُهُمْ مَّا كُنْتُمْ اِيَّانَا تَعْبُدُوْنَ 

**The Polytheists and Their Deities on the Day of Resurrection:** 'And [mention, O Muhammad], the Day We will gather them all together then We will say to those who associated partners [with Us], "[Keep to] your place, you and your partners."' The emphasis in 'all together' (*ǧamīʿan*) shows that He will gather both the polytheists and their deities. Before 'your place' (*makānakum*), a verb is omitted according to the rule of Arabic grammar—namely: *umkuṯū* (stay) or *qifū* (stand). This is used in moments of command to focus the addressee's attention. 'And We will separate them' (*fa-zayyalnā baynahum*)—*tazyīl* means separating and dividing. The Quran states in multiple places that on that Day, He will separate the polytheists from their deities, and their deities will disassociate themselves from them, swearing that they had no knowledge of their worship. This passed under Al-Baqarah 166-167 and Al-An'am 94.

**The Deities' Declaration of Disassociation:** 'and their partners will say, "You did not use to worship us."' This declaration will humiliate the polytheists. They expected help, but their deities will swear: we had no knowledge of your worship. 'And sufficient is Allah as a witness...' is used as an oath, and the particle *an* in *in kunnā* is contracted from *inna*, followed by the emphasizing particle *lā* on *la-ġāfilīn*.

هُنَالِكَ تَبْلُوْا كُلُّ نَفْسٍ مَّآ اَسْلَفَتْ وَرُدُّوْٓا اِلَى اللّٰهِ مَوْلٰىهُمُ الْحَقِّ وَضَلَّ عَنْهُمْ مَّا كَانُوْا يَفْتَرُوْنَ 

**Everyone Will Face Their Own Deeds:** *Balā/Yablū* means experiencing, testing, or tasting. That is: the polytheists expect that their deities will save them, but they will face only their own deeds, and must bear their consequences. 'And they will be returned to Allah, their true Master, and lost from them is what they used to invent.' They expected their deities to welcome them, but those deities they invented will disappear, and their presentation will be before Allah, their true Master. 'Master' (*mawlā*) means the return, and 'true Master' is used in opposition to their false deities.

قُلْ مَنْ يَّرْزُقُكُمْ مِّنَ السَّمَاۗءِ وَالْاَرْضِ اَمَّنْ يَّمْلِكُ السَّمْعَ وَالْاَبْصَارَ وَمَنْ يُّخْرِجُ الْـحَيَّ مِنَ الْمَيِّتِ وَيُخْرِجُ الْمَيِّتَ مِنَ الْحَيِّ وَمَنْ يُّدَبِّرُ الْاَمْرَ ۭ فَسَيَقُوْلُوْنَ اللّٰهُ ۚ فَقُلْ اَفَلَا تَتَّقُوْنَ ۔ فَذٰلِكُمُ اللّٰهُ رَبُّكُمُ الْحَقُّ ۚ فَمَاذَا بَعْدَ الْحَقِّ اِلَّا الضَّلٰلُ ښ فَاَنّٰى تُصْرَفُوْنَ 

**The Contradiction in the Polytheists' Thought:** The words and segments of this verse were discussed elsewhere. Its order is also clear. We explained that the Arabs acknowledged Allah as the Creator, رازق, and Director of the universe, yet chose polytheism. They did not believe their deities were creators of the heavens and the earth, or had power over life, death, and direction, but believed they were His beloved ones, who would intercede for them to obtain worldly blessings and salvation in the Hereafter. The Quran used their own acknowledged premises to show that they have no excuse to deny the Hereafter or associate partners with Him: when He alone has power over life, death, and direction, why does He need partners, and what difficulty does He face in repeating creation? Stating 'Then will you not fear Him?' (*afalā tattaqūna*)—meaning: when you acknowledge this, do you not fear His anger? 'For that is Allah, your Lord, the Truth. And what can be beyond the truth except error?'—the logical consequence of your acknowledgment is that you worship Him alone, yet you chose other lords; this is error. *Tusrafūna* (you are turned away) is in the passive voice, indicating: your reason does not support this; on whose command are you choosing this path?

**The Nature of the Arabs' Polytheism:** Although this is clear, since people's knowledge regarding the Arabs' beliefs is سرسری, a point worth clarifying: the Arabs acknowledged Allah as the Creator, رازق, and Director, but believed He had entrusted certain powers to His beloved ones, just as a king entrusts powers to his ministers. The Quran refuted this concept. This shows that the polytheism of modern times is similar to that of the Arabs; we merely pretend our polytheism is monotheism by magnifying the Arabs' polytheism, whereas the reality is: *this sin is committed in your city too.*

كَذٰلِكَ حَقَّتْ كَلِمَتُ رَبِّكَ عَلَي الَّذِيْنَ فَسَقُوْٓا اَنَّهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُوْنَ 

**The Divine Law Concerning Guidance and Misguidance:** 'Thus' (*ka-ḏālika*) points to their contradictory method, and 'the word of your Lord' (*kalimatu rabbika*) refers to the divine law concerning guidance and misguidance, which was explained in multiple places: Allah opens the path of faith to those who use their intellect; as for those who follow their desires, He leaves them to their deviation. This law has seized them, and they will not believe. This is a shift of focus toward the Prophet, consoling him that their conduct must not grieve him, as they are under His law.

قُلْ هَلْ مِنْ شُرَكَاۗىِٕكُمْ مَّنْ يَّبْدَءُ الْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيْدُهٗ ۭ قُلِ اللّٰهُ يَبْدَءُ الْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيْدُهٗ فَاَنّٰى تُؤْفَكُوْنَ 

**Refuting Their Belief in Intercession:** This refutes their belief that their deities will save them in the Hereafter. We explained that the Arabs were in hesitation regarding the Hereafter, considering it highly improbable; hence, they assumed: if Resurrection occurs, our deities will be our helpers. He commanded: ask them if any of their deities began creation or will repeat it; and when only God will repeat it, your return will be to Him, not to those who had no role in it. 'How are you deluded?' (*annā tu'fakūna*)—meaning: when you acknowledge that only He begins and repeats creation, you must acknowledge that He alone decides; yet you choose contrary views.

قُلْ هَلْ مِنْ شُرَكَاۗىِٕكُمْ مَّنْ يَّهْدِيْٓ اِلَى الْاَخْيَلِ ۭ قُلِ اللّٰهُ يَهْدِيْ لِلْحَقِّ ۭ اَفَمَنْ يَّهْدِيْٓ اِلَى الْاَخْيَلِ اَحَقُّ اَنْ يُّتَّبَعَ اَمَّنْ لَّا يَهِدِّيْٓ اِلَّآ اَنْ يُّهْدٰى ۚ فَمَا لَكُمْ ۣ كَيْفَ تَحْكُمُوْنَ 

**The Deities' Role Regarding Guidance:** In the Hereafter, they have no role; now, what benefit do you receive from them in this world? A creature's greatest need is that the Creator guides him; can you say your deities guide you? Did they grant you your intellect? Did they reveal any scripture, or send any messenger? If they did nothing, why do you follow them? Is the one who guides to the truth more deserving of being followed, or the one who cannot guide unless he is guided? What has happened to you, how do you judge? 

Guidance is used here with both *ilā* and *li*. In our view, *ilā* denotes pointing to a thing, while *li* includes granting the facilitation of guidance. *Yahiddī* is originally *yahtadī*, which became *yahiddī* through assimilation (*idġām*).

وَمَا يَتَّبِعُ اَكْثَرُهُمْ اِلَّا ظَنًّا ۭ اِنَّ الظَّنَّ لَا يُغْنِيْ مِنَ الْحَقِّ شَـيْــــًٔـا ۭ اِنَّ اللّٰهَ عَلِيْمٌۢ بِمَا يَفْعَلُوْنَ 

**Following Mere Assumption:** 'And most of them follow not except assumption. Indeed, assumption avails not against the truth at all.' 'Assumption' (*thann*) is used as the contrary of knowledge, certainty, and truth. The meaning is: they follow mere guesses, which cannot stand in place of the truth. When reality appears, they will realize they built on sand. 'Indeed, Allah is Knowing of what they do' contains a threat to them and consolation to the Prophet. He mentioned 'most of them' (*aktharuhum*) because some among them were thinkers; see verses 42-43.

The upcoming subject (Verses 37 to 70): This sequence began with refuting their demand to change the Quran. Instead of accepting their demand, Allah made the verses they disliked clearer and more detailed. Now, He proved that the Quran is the Book of Allah, consoling the Prophet and his companions. Recite the verses:

وَمَا كَانَ هٰذَا الْقُرْاٰنُ اَنْ يُّفْتَرٰي مِنْ دُوْنِ اللّٰهِ وَلٰكِنْ تَصْدِيْقَ الَّذِيْ بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ وَتَفْصِيْلَ الْكِتٰبِ لَا رَيْبَ فِيْهِ مِنْ رَّبِّ الْعٰلَمِيْنَ ۔ اَمْ يَــقُوْلُوْنَ افْتَرٰىهُ ۭ قُلْ فَاْتُوْا بِسُوْرَةٍ مِّثْلِهٖ وَادْعُوْا مَنِ اسْتَــطَعْتُمْ مِّنْ دُوْنِ اللّٰهِ اِنْ كُنْتُمْ صٰدِقِيْنَ ۔ بَلْ كَذَّبُوْا بِمَا لَمْ يُحِيْطُوْا بِعِلْمِهٖ وَلَمَّا يَاْتِهِمْ تَاْوِيْلُهٗ ۭ كَذٰلِكَ كَذَّبَ الَّذِيْنَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ فَانْظُرْ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَاقِبَةُ الظّٰلِمِيْنَ 

**The Quran Confirms Previous Prophecies:** 'And it was not [possible] for this Quran to be produced by other than Allah, but [it is] a confirmation of what was before it and a detailed explanation of the Book, [about] which there is no doubt, from the Lord of the worlds.' In verse 15, the proof was based on the Prophet's character; now, it is based on the Quran itself. He stated: this is no composition invented and attributed to God, but is the confirmation of those prophecies present in previous scriptures, which we cited in Al-Baqarah and Ali 'Imran. In 'a detailed explanation of the Book' (*tafṣīla al-kitāb*), 'the Book' refers to the Torah, whose brief and ambiguous odes were explained and completed by the Quran. *Lā rayba fīhi min rabbi al-ʿālamīn* means: there is no doubt that it is from the Lord of the worlds.

**The Quran's General Challenge:** 'Or do they say, "He invented it"? Say, "Then bring a surah like it and call upon [for help] whomever you can besides Allah, if you should be truthful."' If they claim he invented it, tell them: then bring a surah like it, seeking the help of your poets, magicians, jinn, and deities. If you are true, call them now when your religion and their divinity are in danger.

**The Real Cause of Their Denial:** 'Nay, they have denied that which they encompass not in knowledge and whose interpretation has not yet come to them.' This points to the real cause of their denial: they deny the punishment of which they are warned because they have not seen it. They want to see it with their eyes before they believe. He stated: this is what previous nations did, and they faced their end. 'Interpretation' (*ta'wīl*) is used here in the sense of the practical fulfillment of a report.

40-44: وَمِنْهُمْ مَّنْ يُّؤْمِنُ بِهٖ وَمِنْهُمْ مَّنْ لَّا يُؤْمِنُ بِهٖ ۭ وَرَبُّكَ اَعْلَمُ بِالْمُفْسِذِيْنَ ۔ وَاِنْ كَذَّبُوْكَ فَقُلْ لِّيْ عَمَلِيْ وَلَكُمْ عَمَلُكُمْ ۚ اَنْتُمْ بَرِيْقُوْنَ مِمَّآ اَعْمَلُ وَاَنَا بَرِيْقٌ مِّمَّا تَعْمَلُوْنَ ۔ وَمِنْهُمْ مَّنْ يَّسْتَمِعُوْنَ اِلَيْكَ ۭاَفَاَنْتَ تُسْمِعُ الصُّمَّ وَلَوْ كَانُوْا لَا يَعْقِلُوْنَ ۔ وَمِنْهُمْ مَّن *يَّنْظُرُ اِلَيْكَ ۭ اَفَاَنْتَ تَهْدِي الْعُمْيَ وَلَوْ كَانُوْا لَا يُبْصِرُوْنَ ۔ اِنَّ اللّٰهَ لَا يَظْلِمُ النَّاسَ شَيْـــًٔـا وَّلٰكِنَّ النَّاسَ اَنْفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُوْنَ 

**The Reason for Granting Respite to the Deniers:** 'And of them are those who believe in it and of them are those who do not believe in it. And your Lord is most knowing of the corrupters.' This is consolation to the Prophet and a threat to the deniers. The meaning is: since some among them are believing, it shows that some possess capacity. For this reason, they are granted respite, so those who are good are selected, and the corrupters are separated. Your Lord is aware of them, and He will punish them.

**Declaration of Disassociation From the Deniers:** 'And if they deny you, then say, "For me are my deeds, and for you are your deeds. You are disassociated from what I do, and I am disassociated from what you do."' This is a command to the Prophet to declare disassociation from those who refuse to understand. We explained elsewhere that this declaration of disassociation from the deniers is the preface to the punishment. As long as the Prophet is among them, he is their security; once he declares disassociation, security is lifted, and punishment is expected. This is why this declaration is highly significant in the life of a nation.

**Consolation to the Prophet:** 'And among them are those who listen to you. But can you make the deaf hear, even though they do not use reason? And among them are those who look at you. But can you guide the blind, even though they do not see?' This is consolation to the Prophet: those who possess hearing and insight listen to you. As for those who became deaf and blind because of their deeds, they will not hear. If you desire that they also believe, this will not occur. The divine law concerning guidance is unchanging; He guides only those who use their capacities.

**A Rectification:** 'Indeed, Allah does not wrong the people at all, but it is the people who wrong themselves.' This is like a rectification: why do people become blind and deaf? He stated: because they wrong themselves. Allah blessed man with the best capacities; if he used them, he would recognize the Prophet's words as the voice of his heart; but they followed their desires, becoming blind and deaf.

45-46: وَيَوْمَ يَحْشُرُهُمْ كَاَنْ لَّمْ يَلْبَثُوْٓا اِلَّا سَاعَةً مِّنَ النَّهَارِ يَتَعَارَفُوْنَ بَيْنَهُمْ ۭ قَدْ خَسِرَ الَّذِيْنَ كَذَّبُوْا بِلِقَاءِ اللّٰهِ وَمَا كَانُوْا مُهْتَدِيْنَ ۔ وَاِمَّا نُرِيَنَّكَ بَعْضَ الَّذِيْ نَعِدُهُمْ اَوْ نَتَوَفَّيَنَّكَ فَاِلَيْنَا مَرْجِعُهُمْ ثُمَّ اللّٰهُ شَهِيْدٌ عَلٰي مَا يَفْعَلُوْنَ 

**Answering Their Haste for Punishment:** This is the answer to their haste. Today, they consider the Hereafter distant; but on the Day they are gathered, they will feel as if they had remained in this world only for an hour. 'they will recognize each other' (*yataʿārafūna baynahum*)—meaning: they will recognize one another as if their meeting was yesterday, and everything will be fresh. The meaning is: the real thing is the feeling they will harbor on that Day; therefore, it is their loss that they considered this world long to ignore the Hereafter, demanding: if it is true, why does it not appear?

'And whether We show you some of what We promise them or at once We cause you to die - then to Us is their return, and Allah is Witness over what they do.' This addresses the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): if His wisdom demands, We will show you some of the punishment during your life; or We will cause you to die, and their return will be to Us, and He will place their deeds before them.

**Why the Decisive Punishment Did Not Fall Upon the Quraysh:** Here, keep His law in view: if a nation denies its messenger, plotting his murder, after His messenger's emigration, He destroys them; but if the believers are a substantial number—as was the case with the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him)—He does not send a physical punishment, but they are destroyed in clash with the believers, and their final decision is in the Hereafter. This is what is pointed to here.

'And for every nation is a messenger. So when their messenger comes, it is judged between them in justice, and they are not wronged.' This warns the Quraysh in the light of His law: He sent His messengers to complete the proof; they served as His court, deciding between truth and falsehood—the believers attained success, and the deniers were eliminated. The meaning is: this stage is before the Quraysh. His scale of justice is set, and His Messenger has come. If you deny him, you will face the same end faced by others. 'and they are not wronged' (*wa-hum lā yuṯlamūna*) shows that He commits no injustice. His Messenger completes the proof, and they are granted full opportunity to understand. This is His law regarding the messengers; it does not apply to the prophets.

48-49: وَيَقُوْلُوْنَ مَتٰى هٰذَا الْوَعْدُ اِنْ كُنْتُمْ صٰدِقِيْنَ ۔ قُلْ لَّآ اَمْلِكُ لِنَفْسِيْ ضَرًّا وَّلَا نَفْعًا اِلَّا مَا شَاۗءَ اللّٰهُ ۭ لِكُلِّ اُمَّةٍ اَجَلٌ ۭاِذَا جَاۗءَ اَجَلُهُمْ فَلَا يَسْتَاْخِرُوْنَ سَاعَةً وَّلَا يَسْتَقْدِمُوْنَ 

**Answering Their Haste:** When they are warned of their end, instead of being alert, they mock, asking: if you are true, when will this promise appear? How long will you repeat this? He commanded: say that this is from God, and I merely warn you; as for when it will come, I have no knowledge of the unseen, nor do I have power over benefit or harm for myself. This is in His authority. When His wisdom demands, it will appear, and no one will escape.

'For every nation is a [specified] term. When their term has come, they will not remain behind an hour, nor will they precede [it].' This is the fundamental answer: every nation has a specified term, and when it comes, they will not remain behind nor precede it. We explained elsewhere that the term of a nation's destruction is measured on His moral scale. He grants them respite until their transgression reaches its designated limit, and He alone knows when the hand reaches the final mark.

50-52: قُلْ اَرَءَيْتُمْ اِنْ اَتٰىكُمْ عَذَابُهٗ بَيَاتًا اَوْ نَهَارًا مَّاذَا يَسْتَعْجِلُ مِنْهُ الْمُجْرِمُوْنَ ۔ اَثُمَّ اِذَا مَا وَقَعَ اٰمَنْتُمْ بِهٖ ۭ اٰۗلْئٰنَ وَقَدْ كُنْتُمْ بِهٖ تَسْتَعْجِلُوْنَ ۔ ثُمَّ قِيْلَ لِلَّذِيْنَ ظَلَمُوْا ذُوْقُوْا عَذَابَ الْخُلْدِ ۚ هَلْ تُجْزَوْنَ اِلَّا بِمَا كُنْتُمْ تَكْسِبُوْنَ 

**A Question to Those Who Hasten:** He asked them: on what strength do you demand the punishment? Whether it falls by night or by day, what defense have you prepared? Stating 'the criminals' (*al-muǧrimūna*) subtly indicates that while there is some justification for those free of crime to ask, your asking while being the criminals is proof of your misfortune. 'Then is it that when it has actually occurred you will believe in it?'—do you demand it so you believe when it appears? But what benefit will faith show then? You will be told: now you believe, when you used to hasten it, taking it for a mere bluff! 'Then it will be said to those who wronged...'—after the worldly punishment, when the punishment of the Hereafter arrives, they will be told: now taste the eternal punishment; this is the fruit of your own deeds. They are termed 'those who wronged' (*alladhīna thalamū*) because they sowed this harvest for themselves. God committed no injustice against them.

53-54: وَيَسْتَنْۢبِـــُٔـوْنَكَ اَحَقٌّ هُوَ ڼ قُلْ اِيْ وَرَبِّيْٓ اِنَّهٗ لَحَقٌّ ې وَمَآ اَنْتُمْ بِمُعْجِزِيْنَ ۔ وَلَوْ اَنَّ لِكُلِّ نَفْسٍ ظَلَمَتْ مَا فِي الْاَرْضِ لَافْـــتَدَتْ بِهٖ ۭ وَاَسَرُّوا النَّدَامَةَ لَمَّا رَاَوُا الْعَذَابَ ۚ وَقُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ بِالْقِسْطِ وَهُمْ لَا يُظْلَمُوْنَ 

'And they ask you to inform them, "Is it true?"' This is similar to 'when will this promise appear' above, but here, the mockery is open, asked by those who insolently ask: is this punishment true, or are you merely boasting? 'Say, "Yes, by my Lord, indeed, it is true; and you cannot cause failure."' *Ī* is used only before an oath. He did not confine Himself to a simple answer, but swore an oath that this is true, adding 'and you cannot cause failure' (*wa-mā antum bi-muʿǧizīna*) to show they cannot escape.

'And if each soul that wronged had everything on earth, it would [offer to] ransom itself with it.' That is: today you mock it, but when it appears, every soul that wronged itself will offer everything on earth to escape. 'And they will confide regret when they see the punishment' describes the reality mentioned in Surah Al-An'am, verse 31: 'until, when the Hour comes upon them unexpectedly, they will say, "Oh, [how great is] our regret over what we neglected."' Since regret has its source in the inner self, the word 'confide' (*asarrū*) was used. The meaning is: they will regret their foolishness. 'and they will be judged in justice, and they will not be wronged'—meaning: they will be judged in justice, and what they sowed will be placed before them. This also indicates that their deities will not save them; nothing will affect His justice.

55-56: اَلَآ اِنَّ لِلّٰهِ مَا فِي السَّمٰوٰتِ وَالْاَرْضِ ۭ اَلَآ اِنَّ وَعْدَ اللّٰهِ حَقٌّ وَّلٰكِنَّ اَكْثَرَهمْ لَا يَعْلَمُوْنَ ۔ هُوَ يُـحْيٖ وَيُمِيْتُ وَاِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُوْنَ ۔ This emphasizes the preceding subject through monotheism, warning them: hear well, everything in the heavens and the earth belongs to God alone; His promise and threat are real and true. He gives life, He causes death, and to Him is your return. If anyone assumed their deities would save them, let them reform this thought. Those who have no power over life or death cannot be the return in the Hereafter. 'but most of them do not know' contains grief and sorrow: this great reality is before them, yet they close their ears to it.

57-58: يٰٓاَيُّهَا النَّاسُ قَدْ جَاۗءَتْكُمْ مَّوْعِظَةٌ مِّنْ رَّبِّكُمْ وَشِفَاۗءٌ لِّمَا فِي الصُّدُوْرِ ڏ وَهُدًى وَّرَحْمَةٌ لِّلْمُؤْمِنِيْنَ ۔ قُلْ بِفَضْلِ اللّٰهِ وَبِرَحْمَتِهٖ فَبِذٰلِكَ فَلْيَفْرَحُوْا ۭ هُوَ خَيْرٌ مِّمَّا يَجْمَعُوْنَ 

'O mankind, there has come to you an instruction...' This returned to the Quran itself, describing four of its attributes to show what a great favor they are receiving, yet they do not appreciate it. First, it is 'an instruction' (*mawʿiẓatun*)—warning people against dangers; second, it is 'a healing for what is in the breasts' (*šifā'un limā fī al-ṣudūri*)—curing those diseases of the heart that make a person like a beast; third and fourth, it is 'guidance and mercy' (*hudan wa-rahmatun*), which cover both this world and the Hereafter.

'Say, "In the bounty of Allah and in His mercy - in that let them rejoice..."'—a verb is omitted here: say that this Quran came carrying His bounty and mercy; let those who wish to rejoice, rejoice in it. 'it is better than what they hoard'—it is far better than those petty goods of the world they are hoarding.

59-60: قُلْ اَرَءَيْتُمْ مَّآ اَنْزَلَ اللّٰهُ لَكُمْ مِّنْ رِّزْقٍ فَجَــعَلْتُمْ مِّنْهُ حَرَامًا وَّحَلٰلًا ۭ قُلْ اٰۗلله اَذِنَ لَكُمْ اَمْ عَلَي اللّٰهِ تَفْتَرُوْنَ ۔ وَمَا ظَنُّ الَّذِيْنَ يَفْتَرُوْنَ عَلَي اللّٰهِ الْكَذِبَ يَوْمَ الْقِيٰمَةِ ۭ اِنَّ اللّٰهَ لَذُوْ فَضْلٍ عَلَي النَّاسِ وَلٰكِنَّ اَكْثَرَهمْ لَا يَشْكُرُوْنَ 

'Say, "Have you seen what Allah has sent down for you of provision..."'—declaring things lawful or unlawful arbitrarily is an interference in His authority, which is polytheism. They did so based on their pagan concepts, claiming: 'this is God's command.' Attributing their innovations to God is a great slander. 'And what will be the assumption of those who invent lies against Allah on the Day of Resurrection?'—this is a threat: do they take this great crime as a minor thing? They will face His punishment. 'Indeed, Allah is full of bounty to the people'—He grants them respite and provision despite their crimes, so they may reform; 'but most of them do not show gratitude,' committing more mischief.

61-65: وَمَا تَكُوْنُ فِيْ شَاْنٍ وَّمَا تَتْلُوْا مِنْهُ مِنْ قُرْاٰنٍ وَّلَا تَعْمَلُوْنَ مِنْ عَمَلٍ اِلَّا كُنَّا عَلَيْكُمْ شُهُوْدًا اِذْ تُفِيْضُوْنَ فِيْهِ ۭ وَمَا يَعْزُبُ عَنْ رَّبِّكَ مِنْ مِّثْقَالِ ذَرَّةٍ فِي الْاَرْضِ وَلَا فِي السَّمَاۗءِ وَلَآ اَصْغَرَ مِنْ ذٰلِكَ وَلَآ اَکْبَرَ اِلَّا فِيْ كِتٰبٍ مُّبِيْنٍ 

**Consolation to the Prophet and His Companions:** This consoles the Prophet and his companions: pay no heed to their plots. Whichever state you are in, and whichever portion of the Quran you are reciting, and whichever deed you are performing, We are witnesses over you. Nothing is hidden from God; everything is written in a clear register. In 'And you do not recite of it any portion of the Quran' (*wa-mā tatlū minhu min qur'ānin*), the pronoun returns to the Book of Allah, and 'Quran' refers to some portion of it. In 'and you do not do any deed,' the address is plural, to include the companions who were sacrificing their lives in His cause. *Afāḍa fī* means being fully absorbed. This shows that when you are fully absorbed in elevating His word, We are protecting and supervising you.

'Unquestionably, [for] the allies of Allah there will be no fear concerning them, nor will they grieve'—this is the group of His allies, who will face no fear or grief in the Hereafter.

'Those who believed and used to fear Allah.' This shows that His alliance is conditional upon faith and piety. It is not the monopoly of any group.

'For them are good tidings in the worldly life and in the Hereafter. No change is there in the words of Allah. That is what is the great success.' They have the glad tiding of dominance in this world, and of Paradise in the Hereafter. His promises are certain, and this is the great success. 'And let not their speech grieve you. Indeed, honor [belongs] to Allah entirely. He is the Hearing, the Knowing.' The glad tiding of dominance could be mocked by the disbelievers, as the Muslims were poor and weak; hence, He commanded: let not their speech grieve you; honor belongs to Allah alone, who will grant it to you. He is Hearing and Knowing; He hears their words, and will judge them.

66-70: اَلَآ اِنَّ لِلّٰهِ مَنْ فِي السَّمٰوٰتِ وَمَنْ فِي الْاَرْضِ ۭ وَمَا يَتَّبِعُ الَّذِيْنَ يَدْعُوْنَ مِنْ دُوْنِ اللّٰهِ شُرَكَاۗءَ ۭ اِنْ يَّتَّبِعُوْنَ اِلَّا الظَّنَّ وَاِنْ همْ اِلَّا يَخْرُصُوْنَ 

This returned to the subject of monotheism: hear well, everyone in the heavens and the earth is His creature and property; none is a partner in His divinity. Those who invoke others besides Allah do not follow true partners, because He has no partner; they follow mere assumptions and guesses.

'It is He who made for you the night to rest therein and the day giving sight. Indeed in that are signs for a people who listen.' Some words are omitted here according to the rule of Arabic grammar—namely: He made the night dark to rest, and the day bright to work. He stated: the alternation of night and day contains signs for those who listen. First, it shows that all opposites serve a single, wise Creator, which is the proof of monotheism. Second, it shows His power and wisdom, which is the proof of the Hereafter. Third, His system of lordship necessitates that we are responsible to Him, and will be questioned regarding His rights.

'They have said, "Allah has taken a son." Exalted is He! He is the [one] Free of need. To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth. You have no authority for this. Do you say about Allah that which you do not know?' *Walad* (son/child) is used as singular, plural, masculine, and feminine. The Arabs called the angels the daughters of God. *Subhānahu*—this is contrary to His divinity, because a child must be of His essence and similar to Him, which is contrary to His uniqueness. 'He is the Free of need'—man needs children to preserve his generation and help him; He is free of need. 'To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens...'—why does He need a child? 'You have no authority' (*in ʿindakum min sulṭānin*)—meaning: you have no proof, but commit a slander.

'Say, "Indeed, those who invent lies against Allah will not succeed."' They will be deprived of success in the Hereafter.

'[It is but] a brief enjoyment in this world; then to Us is their return; then We will make them taste the severe punishment because they used to disbelieve.' They will enjoy this world for a few days, and then face Our severe punishment.

The upcoming subject (Verses 71 to 93): Next, the history of Noah and Moses is described in some detail, while other messengers are mentioned briefly. The purpose is to verify through history those realities explained above through reason and nature. This contains consolation for the Prophet and his companions, and lessons for the opponents. Recite the verses:

'And recite to them the news of Noah, when he said to his people, "O my people, if my residence and my reminding of the signs of Allah has become detestable to you - then upon Allah I have relied. So resolve upon your plan and [call] your partners. Then let not your plan be an ambiguity for you. Then carry it out upon me and do not give me respite."' 

Noah is the first whose history of destruction was well known among the Arabs; therefore, he is mentioned first. *Naba'* refers to an important event; this shows that the purpose is to narrate the decisive event of his life, not a mere historical story. 'if my residence and my reminding... has become detestable to you'—'residence' (*maqāmī*) refers to his long stay among them, of which his opponents became tired. This is the introduction to his challenge.

**Noah's Challenge:** 'So resolve upon your plan...'—'plan' (*amr*) means decision. That is: make a final decision regarding me, including your deities in it. 'Then let not your plan be an ambiguity for you.' *Ġummah* means an ambiguity or hesitation; meaning: make a decisive decision. 'Then carry it out upon me and do not give me respite.'—*aqḍū ilayya* means: carry out the decision upon me without any delay. My trust is in Allah, who will protect me.

'And if you turn away [from my advice] - then no payment have I asked of you. My reward is only from Allah, and I have been commanded to be of the Muslims.' That is: if you choose to turn away, I do not care. I did not ask for any reward for my advice; my reward is from Allah. My duty was not to force you to believe, but to be obedient (*Muslim*) to my Lord.

'But they denied him, so We saved him and those with him in the ship and made them successors, and We drowned those who denied Our signs. Then see how was the end of those who were warned.' First, He mentioned the salvation of the believers, because His law is to save the believers before sending the punishment. 'and made them successors'—He destroyed the deniers, giving the inheritance of the land to the believers. 'Then see how was the end...'—this is consolation to the Prophet: just as Noah's deniers were destroyed, the Quraysh will be destroyed if they do not learn a lesson.

'Then We sent after him messengers to their peoples, and they came to them with clear proofs. But they were not to believe in what they had denied before. Thus We seal the hearts of the transgressors.' This is a brief reference to those messengers who came after Noah, while detailing their history in the subsequent Surah (Surah Hud). 'But they were not to believe...' points to His law: those who deny clear proofs are deprived of the facilitation of faith. 'Transgressors' (*al-mu'tadīn*) are those who break His limits, losing the capacity to respect them, which is why they never wake.

'Then We sent after them Moses and Aaron to Pharaoh and his establishment with Our signs, but they were arrogant and were a criminal people.' *Mala'* means the establishment and leaders, and 'signs' (*āyāt*) refers to the verses and miracles. 'and were a criminal people' points to their moral death, because of which they did not benefit from His signs. It should be kept in mind that Moses and Aaron went as messengers; therefore, they completed the proof. This refutes the view that Moses merely demanded the freedom of the Israelites without inviting Pharaoh to Islam.

Commentary on Verses 76-77:

'So when there came to them the truth from Us, they said, "Indeed, this is an obvious magic."' 'The truth' (*al-haqq*) means a clear, proven reality—namely: their claim to be the messengers of Allah, supported by miracles. The disbelievers denied it, calling it magic. 'Moses said, "Do you say [thus] of the truth when it has come to you? Is this magic? But magicians will not succeed."' In 'Do you say of the truth...', the object is omitted out of dislike—meaning: you call the truth magic! 'when it has come to you'—one can be excused when the truth is hidden; but challenging it when it is clear is kutter-obstinacy. We explained that the difference between magic and a miracle is clear from their appearance, impact, and the character of their bringers. Magicians never succeed; their tricks are temporary, and they cannot stand before the light of a miracle. This is a challenge: when you bring your magicians, you will see the difference.

**A Political Accusation Against Moses:** 'They said, "Have you come to us to turn us away from that upon which we found our fathers and so that greatness [i.e., leadership] in the land may be for you two? And we are not believers in you."' This is a highly malicious accusation: to incite their people, they accused Moses and Aaron of conspiring against their ancestral religion and seeking political power. We explained that the establishment was fearful of the Israelites' growing numbers, carrying out the killing of their infants to control them; therefore, they fanned this rumor to incite قبطی bias against Moses.

Commentary on Verses 79-82:

'And Pharaoh said, "Bring to me every learned magician."' Pharaoh knew Moses was not a magician, but to protect his status, he chose to bring his magicians to face Moses, which was a foolish attempt. 

'So when the magicians came, Moses said to them, "Throw down whatever you will throw."' Allowing them to throw first shows his absolute confidence in His help, and this was his first victory.

'So when they had thrown, Moses said, "What you have brought is [only] magic. Indeed, Allah will expose its worthlessness. Indeed, Allah does not amend the work of corrupters."' This is the answer to their 'this is obvious magic' in verse 76: you called my miracle magic; magic is what you brought, and Allah will expose its worthlessness. Falsehood disappears when faced with the truth. 'Indeed, Allah does not amend the work of corrupters'—meaning: His law is that His help does not make the work of corrupters successful; victory belongs only to the truth.

'And Allah will establish the truth by His words, even if the criminals dislike it.' Allah will establish His truth through His words, despite the criminals' opposition.

'But no one believed Moses except some youths of his people, for fear of Pharaoh and his establishment lest he persecute them. And indeed, Pharaoh was haughty upon the earth, and indeed, he was of the transgressors.'

**The Early Companions of Moses:** 'youths' (*ḏurriyyatun*) does not denote only small numbers, but shows that those who first believed in Moses were several youths. The law of God is that in the beginning, only the youths support the prophets, because accepting the call requires courage and facing dangers, which is difficult for those bound by customs and interests. The youths are free from this, which is why they advance. Under Pharaoh, who was haughty and crossed all limits in oppression, only those who possessed high courage could support Moses.

**Difference Between 'Amana Lahu' and 'Amana Bihi':** Their belief is expressed through *āmana lahu*. In Arabic, *āmana lahu* means accepting someone's claim, without requiring complete submission and obedience; but *āmana bihi* requires complete submission and obedience. At that stage, these youths accepted Moses' claim, but had not reached complete submission; hence, the Prophet taught them the reality of faith in the subsequent verse. 'for fear of Pharaoh and his establishment'—they feared Pharaoh's oppression, and also feared their own elders, who did not wish to face any danger.

Commentary on Verses 84-86:

'And Moses said, "O my people, if you have believed in Allah, then on Him rely, if you should be Muslims."' This is Moses (peace be upon him) teaching his people the reality of faith: if you claim faith, let no fear or regard prevent you, but place your trust in Allah. Piety requires trust, and trust requires complete submission (*Islam*).

'So they said, "Upon Allah we have relied. Our Lord, make us not [objects of] trial for the wrongdoing people."' They accepted this teaching, and knowing that trust requires sacrifice, they supplicated: 'Our Lord, make us not [objects of] trial...' *Fitnah* here means the target. The meaning is: do not let them persecute us to turn us away from our religion. This shows that trust requires constant supplication to God.

'And save us by Your mercy from the disbelieving people.' Since they knew they were to leave, they supplicated for the success of their emigration.

'And We inspired to Moses and his brother, "Settle your people in Egypt in houses and make your houses a place of prayer [i.e., Qiblah] and establish prayer and give good tidings to the believers."' This is the instruction to attain that patience and trust. They were commanded to designate some houses in Egypt as places of prayer, where they could gather to pray, making these houses the Qiblah. This is similar to the instruction given to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) in Mecca.

**The Beginning of Religious Organization:** Under slavery, the Israelites had lost their religious organization, including congregational prayers. Moses began with establishing prayer, designating houses as mosques. Since they had multiple mosques, they needed a Qiblah to integrate them; hence, He commanded: 'and make your houses a place of prayer [i.e., Qiblah]' (*wa-ǧʿalū buyūtakum qiblatan*). This refers to those houses designated for prayer by Moses and Aaron, which served as the Qiblah in this temporary period. After emigration, they made the Ark of the Covenant their Qiblah until the Temple was constructed. 'and give good tidings to the believers' points to the glad tiding in verse 2: 'that they have a foot of truthfulness with their Lord', which is detailed in verse 93.

'And Moses said, "Our Lord, indeed You have given Pharaoh and his establishment splendor and wealth in the worldly life, our Lord, that they may lead [people] astray from Your path. Our Lord, obliterate their wealth and harden their hearts so they will not believe until they see the painful punishment."' Moses supplicated this when he despaired of their faith, just as Noah supplicated: 'My Lord, indeed they have disobeyed me and followed him whose wealth and children will not increase him except in loss... and they have led astray many, and do not increase the wrongdoers except in error... My Lord, do not leave upon the earth from among the disbelievers an inhabitant.'

Once a messenger completes the proof, those who refuse to believe are past cure. Their remaining on earth only leads others astray; hence, His law is to destroy them. Moses made this supplication when he realized that only His punishment would open their eyes. 'our Lord, that they may lead [people] astray...'—the particle *li* (that) denotes the consequence: the wealth He gave them was used by them to lead people astray; therefore, He supplicated: 'Our Lord, obliterate their wealth...' The wrongdoers had demanded to see the punishment before they believed; hence, Moses supplicated for what they demanded.

'[Allah] said, "Your supplication has been answered. So remain on a right course and follow not the way of those who do not know."' The supplication was accepted, and they were commanded: remain firm on your course, and let no compassion or intercession arise in your hearts for them when His punishment falls. This is similar to what He commanded Noah: 'And construct the ship under Our eyes and Our inspiration and do not address Me concerning those who have wronged; indeed, they are to be drowned.' (Hud: 37). It is also similar to what He commanded our Prophet: 'So remain on a right course as you have been commanded, [you] and those who have turned back with you, and do not transgress... And do not incline toward those who do wrong, lest you be touched by the Fire, and you would not have other than Allah any protectors, then you would not be helped.' (Hud: 112-113). This is to warn the leaders of the flock lest any of their sheep join the cursed.

Commentary on Verses 90-92:

'And We took the Children of Israel across the sea, and Pharaoh and his soldiers pursued them in insolence and enmity until, when drowning overtook him, he said, "I believe that there is no deity except that in whom the Children of Israel believe, and I am of the Muslims."' He took them across the sea as if carrying them on His shoulders. How this occurred is discussed where the Quran points to it. This was a miracle: the sea opened a dry path for the Israelites, but drowned Pharaoh and his army. His pursuit is termed 'insolence and enmity' (*baġyan wa-ʿadwan*). After granting them permission to leave, pursuing them was the greatest of his injustices. His declaration of faith 'I believe that there is no deity except...' shows the helplessness of tyrants when caught by His punishment.

'Now? And you had disobeyed before and were of the corrupters.' Now you believe, when the time of faith has passed, and your entire life was spent in corruption! This is a description of his state, not necessarily spoken in words.

'So today We will save you in body that you may be to those who succeed you a sign. And indeed, many among the people, of Our signs, are heedless.' His body was cast out by the sea to serve as a sign of lesson for others. Although experts differ on whether the mummy in Cairo is of this Pharaoh, the Quran's statement is more reliable.
