His body remains as a sign of lesson, but there are few who learn: 'And indeed, many among the people, of Our signs, are heedless.'

'And We settled the Children of Israel in a settlement of truthfulness [i.e., home of security] and provided them with good things. And they did not differ until knowledge came to them. Indeed, your Lord will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection concerning that over which they used to differ.' *Tabwi'ah* means settling, and 'truthfulness' (*ṣidq*) highlights its firmness and security—meaning: He settled them in the Promised Land (Jordan and Syria). 'and provided them with good things' points to the fertility of that region, as detailed in the Torah. 'And they did not differ until knowledge came to them' points to their ingratitude: they created differences in His religion, losing the truth and falling into confusion. Stating 'they did not differ until knowledge came to them' shows that they stumbled in the full light of day; hence, the responsibility lies on them. 'Indeed, your Lord will judge...' is a threat to the Jews: He will judge all their differences on the Day of Resurrection.

The upcoming subject (Verses 94 to 109): the remaining part of the Surah serves as a conclusion. First, He addressed the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): their denial must not grieve you; those who read the Book before will confirm this truth. Then He consoled him: those under His law will not believe, even if shown every sign; they believe only when the punishment falls, when faith is useless. In history, only the people of Jonah are an exception, who believed before the punishment and were saved. Allah desires that people believe of choice, not by force; hence, He does not encourage their demands for miracles. Let those who seek signs reflect on the heavens and the earth, and those who seek the punishment wait. Finally, He commanded the Prophet to declare absolute disassociation from their deities, so they completely despair. Recite the verses:

Commentary on Verses 94-95:

'So if you are in doubt, [O Muhammad], about that which We have revealed to you, then ask those who have been reading the Scripture before you. The truth has certainly come to you from your Lord, so never be among the doubters. And never be of those who deny the signs of Allah and [thus] be among the losers.' Sometimes, the address is to the Prophet, but the focus is on the deniers, who do not deserve to be addressed directly. Facing general opposition can produce some temporary reflection in a person. To protect the Prophet, He warned: let not their denial cause you any doubt. 'then ask those who have been reading the Scripture before you'—if those ignorant of the Book deny it, ask those who have been reading the Book, for they will confirm it. 'Those' refers to the sincere among the People of the Book, whom the Quran referenced in many places. This also encouraged them to introduce this truth.

'And never be...' is addressed to the Prophet, but the focus is on the deniers. A similar consolation is in Surah Hud: 'So is one who [stands] upon a clear document from his Lord, and a witness from Him recites it, and before it was the Scripture of Moses to lead and as a mercy? Those believe in it. But whoever disbelieves in it of the [various] groups - the Fire is his promised destination. So be not in doubt about it. Indeed, it is the truth from your Lord, but most of the people do not believe.' (Hud: 17).

Commentary on Verses 96-97:

'Indeed, those upon whom the word of your Lord has come into effect will not believe, even if every sign should come to them, until they see the painful punishment.' This points to His law mentioned in verse 33: 'Thus the word of your Lord has come into effect upon those who defiantly disobeyed - that they will not believe.'—meaning: those who close their eyes to the truth have their hearts sealed, after which they do not believe.

'even if every sign...'—such people are not affected by any miracle. They believe only when the decisive punishment falls, when their faith is not accepted.

'Then has there not been a [single] city that believed so its faith benefited it except the people of Jonah? When they believed, We removed from them the punishment of disgrace in worldly life and gave them enjoyment for a time.' This consoles the Prophet: no previous nation believed before the punishment; they believed only when it fell, when faith was useless. Only the people of Jonah are an exception, who believed before the punishment fell, and were saved.

This also contains a warning and encouragement for the Quraysh: your term is drawing near; instead of choosing the path of destruction, choose the path of the people of Jonah so you are saved. It should be kept in mind that no physical, decisive punishment fell upon the Quraysh; rather, those who possessed capacity embraced Islam gradually, and only the wicked were eliminated in battles.

Commentary on Verses 99-100:

'And had your Lord willed, those on the earth would have believed - all of them entirely. Then, [O Muhammad], would you compel the people in order that they become believers?' This also consoles the Prophet: do not grieve over their denial; Allah desires that they believe, but of choice, not by force. If He willed force, He would have made everyone believe. Your duty is solely to convey the truth, not to force them.

'And it is not for a soul to believe except by permission of Allah, and He will place defilement upon those who will not use reason.' This describes His law: a person believes only by His permission, and He grants His permission to those who use their intellect. As for those who do not, He places defilement (*al-riǧs*) upon their hearts, which makes them blind.

Commentary on Verses 101-103:

'Say, "Observe what is in the heavens and earth." But of no avail will be signs or warners to a people who do not believe.' This answers their demand for miracles: tell them that the heavens and the earth are filled with miracles; reflect on them, for they are sufficient. Then, He clarified: signs and warnings benefit only those who desire to learn; those who do not wish to believe are benefited by no sign. 'Do not believe' (*lā yu'minūna*) is used in the sense of intending not to believe, and 'warnings' (*nudhur*) refers to those signs He shows to produce His fear.

'So do they wait except for [ones] like the days of those who passed before them? Say, "Then wait; indeed, I am with you among those who wait."' They see no signs in the universe; they wait for a physical punishment like those faced by previous nations. Tell them: if you wait for it, wait; I am also waiting. I have no power to bring it; it is in His authority, but I see its causes accumulating.

'Then We will save Our messengers and those who have believed. Thus, it is an obligation upon Us that We save the believers.' This describes the result of the punishment: when it falls, only the messengers and the believers are saved, while the rest are destroyed. This is a warning to the Quraysh: this law applies to you too, and We will save those who believe.

It should be kept in mind that when a decisive punishment falls, the messengers and the believers are saved; only the deniers are eliminated. But this applies to the decisive punishment; as for general trials, they touch both, though the believers benefit from them.

Commentary on Verses 104-107:

'Say, "O mankind, if you are in doubt as to my religion - then I do not worship those which you worship besides Allah; but I worship Allah, who causes your death. And I have been commanded to be of the believers."' This is the final declaration the Prophet was commanded to make, so the disbelievers completely despair of any compromise. This is the declaration of disassociation, which is followed by emigration. 'who causes your death' (*al-laḏī yatawaffākum*) is a warning: He is the One who causes your death, before whom you must be presented. 'And I have been commanded to be of the believers'—meaning: my duty is to be a believer, regardless of what you choose.

'And [commanded], "Direct your face toward the religion, inclining to truth, and never be of those who associate partners with Allah."' This explains the core of faith: complete focus toward the One God, avoiding all traces of polytheism.

'And do not invoke besides Allah that which neither benefits you nor harms you, for if you did, then indeed you would be of the wrongdoers.' This refutes polytheism: since nothing can benefit or harm without His permission, invoking others besides Allah is a great wrong against oneself, and is violating His greatest right.

'And if Allah should touch you with adversity, there is no remover of it except Him; and if He intends for you good, then there is no repeller of His bounty. He causes it to reach whom He wills of His servants. And He is the Forgiving, the Merciful.' This refutes all those concepts based on which they worshiped their deities. Finally, referencing 'the Forgiving, the Merciful' is to show: since He is the Forgiving and Merciful, let His servants seek His mercy alone, not the support of others.

'Say, "O mankind, the truth has come to you from your Lord, so whoever is guided is only guided for [the benefit of] his soul, and whoever goes astray only goes astray against it. And I am not over you a trustee [i.e., guardian]."'

This is the final warning: this which is presented to you is the truth from your Lord; whoever accepts it does so for his own good, and whoever goes astray does so to his own loss. My duty is solely to convey the truth, not to force you to believe.

'And follow what is revealed to you, [O Muhammad], and be patient until Allah judges. And He is the best of judges.' This is the final instruction to the Prophet: ignore their denial, follow what is revealed to you, and remain firm on your course until Allah judges. This judgment was pointed to in verse 103: He saves His messengers and the believers, while His opponents fail.

These are the final lines of the commentary of this Surah written down today, Friday, at 9:00 AM. May Allah forgive the errors and grant place in the hearts for the true words. And our final call is: Praise be to Allah, Lord of the worlds.
